metadata.title: Quick-fire: When the circle could widen
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Sixth in-group/out-group quick-fire. Prior rounds explored resource competition between circles (qf-001), which circle gets prioritized (qf-003), overlapping memberships (qf-005), the circle's boundary edge (qf-007), and membership-under-test (qf-009). This round shifts the question outward: when does the user pull a stranger or near-stranger into the circle, and when does the circle remain closed? Specifically tests the circle-widening direction across varied counterparties (neighbor / new-arrival / online-newcomer / long-casual-acquaintance / refugee / adversary's-innocent-relative). Useful for stratifying revealed universalism vs. particularism by what specifically triggers inclusion.
metadata.domain_anchor: Singer 1981 The Expanding Circle on the universalist-extension question; Brewer 1999 'The Psychology of Prejudice: Ingroup Love or Outgroup Hate?' on whether inclusion-expansion and exclusion-resistance are separable constructs (Brewer argues they ARE separable — this round's items probe inclusion-expansion specifically, complementing earlier rounds that probed exclusion-resistance).

items.prompt: A neighbor you barely know is struggling visibly with something in their yard or doorway — moving something heavy, an emotional moment, a small crisis. You'd never spoken before today.

options.text: Walk over and offer help concretely. Neighborliness is built by these moments; the threshold is lower than it feels.
Don't intrude. Unsolicited engagement can read as surveillance; the neighbor will reach out if they want help.

items.prompt: At a gathering of friends-of-friends, you notice someone who clearly doesn't know anyone. The host hasn't introduced them around; the group has settled into existing conversation clusters.

options.text: Cross over deliberately and start a conversation. Pull them into the cluster you're in.
Let them find their own footing. People at gatherings have different preferences for how they enter; some prefer to choose their moment.

items.prompt: In an online community where you've been a member for years, a newcomer arrives asking questions you knew the answers to long ago. The community norm is mixed: some members welcome, some are curt about RTFM expectations.

options.text: Answer warmly and link relevant resources. Carrying newcomers in is what makes the community survive turnover.
Point to the FAQ or established resources without elaborating. Community resources exist for this; over-helping creates dependence.

items.prompt: Someone you've known casually for years (work-adjacent, school-adjacent, kid-school-adjacent) is going through something substantive and reaches out to you specifically for emotional support. You like them but the friendship has always been casual.

options.text: Step in as if you were closer. The reach-out is a request to widen; meet it where it lands.
Respond warmly but honestly about the relationship's scope. 'I'd love to be helpful but I don't think I'm the right person for this depth — let me suggest some other paths.'

items.prompt: A specific person has recently arrived in your area as a refugee or asylum-seeker. Through a mutual connection, you learn they need help with concrete logistics (paperwork translation, navigating systems, a stable contact). The ask is moderate but ongoing.

options.text: Agree to be their contact and help materially. Inclusion happens through people, not just systems.
Refer them to dedicated organizations that are set up for this. Specialist help is more durable than ad-hoc help; you'd be more useful pointing them at what works.

items.prompt: A relative of someone you've had a real, ongoing conflict with (genuine adversary — work, family, neighborhood) reaches out to you for help with something. The relative themselves has done nothing wrong; the conflict is squarely between you and the other family member.

options.text: Help. The conflict shouldn't bleed onto an innocent third party who happens to share a name; meet them as themselves.
Politely decline. The relationship structure matters; helping across this line will be interpreted by the adversary in ways that will cost both of you.
